one_line_takeaway: A workshop extended abstract arguing that machine-unlearning privacy is chronically mis-measured, and proposing a benchmarking framework (cheap offline-LiRA MIA, distribution-preserving per-example epsilon, update-leakage attacks, and iterative test-accuracy tracking) whose preliminary CIFAR10/ResNet18 run shows that beating the standard weak MIA does not imply real privacy and that some algorithms (e.g., BadTeach) destroy accuracy under repeated unlearning.
key_finding: In the iterative CIFAR10/ResNet18 setting there are notable test-accuracy discrepancies across algorithms: BadTeach rapidly collapses model performance to random guessing, while other algorithms maintain or even increase test accuracy over iterations (Figure 1). Across the privacy evaluations the authors report that defending successfully against the basic logistic-regression MIA (used in most prior unlearning papers) does NOT necessarily translate to defending against stronger MIAs. For update-leakage, only some tested algorithms perform better than retraining-from-scratch, but none were found to leak extra information (the 'do no harm' criterion held). They also find hyperparameter tuning is decisive and trades off performance against privacy, and that tuning against an expensive attack like online-LiRA can be prohibitively slow for real-world adoption.
core_contribution: This is a short extended abstract (accepted at the Deep Learning Security and Privacy Workshop 2024, co-located with IEEE S&P) that critiques how machine unlearning algorithms are evaluated and proposes an improved benchmarking framework built around three under-tested dimensions. The authors' own contributions are: (1) adapting the OFFLINE version of the Likelihood Ratio Attack (LiRA) to the unlearning setting as a stronger-but-cheaper membership inference attack (MIA) than the ubiquitous logistic-regression loss-classifier MIA, and re-implementing the NeurIPS-2023-competition per-example epsilon estimation so the full distribution of privacy levels is preserved rather than averaged into one score; (2) implementing and running an update-leakage attack (adversary compares base-model vs. unlearned-model outputs) on standard vision unlearning algorithms, which the authors say had not previously been benchmarked for algorithms other than SISA and GraphEraser; and (3) building an iterative unlearning pipeline that measures test-accuracy degradation over repeated unlearning applications, a setting they report has essentially not been evaluated in the vision domain. They demonstrate the framework with a preliminary CIFAR10/ResNet18 study. Note: online-LiRA (from Carlini et al. and its unlearning adaptation by Kurmanji et al.), the update-leakage concept (Chen et al. 'When machine unlearning jeopardizes privacy'), and per-example epsilon (NeurIPS-2023 competition) are CITED prior work; the paper's original piece is the offline-LiRA adaptation, the distribution-preserving epsilon re-implementation, the broader update-leakage benchmarking, and the iterative vision pipeline plus preliminary experiments.
threat_model: Privacy adversary performing membership inference against a model that has undergone unlearning, aiming to determine whether a specific example belonged to the forget set (i.e., whether the 'right to be forgotten' request was truly honored). The paper argues realistic threats require (a) strong attacks (LiRA rather than a simple logistic-regression loss classifier) reported with worst-case metrics (ROC at low false-positive rates), not average-case accuracy/recall; and (b) an update-leakage adversary who has access to BOTH the base model's and the unlearned model's outputs and exploits their difference as an attack vector, which can make unlearning leak MORE than never unlearning. The privacy goal is framed in (epsilon, delta)-differential-privacy terms, using group-level DP to reflect that deletion requests are batched.
limitations: Explicitly preliminary: this is an extended abstract, not a full study. Results shown are limited to a single dataset (CIFAR10) and architecture (ResNet18), chosen for small compute footprint; the authors state in Future Work that they will run more architectures and datasets. The only figure (iterative test accuracy) characterizes neither privacy nor runtime — the paper describes qualitative privacy/update-leakage conclusions in prose but presents no privacy tables or ROC/epsilon-distribution plots in this version. The promised open-source toolkit is future work, not yet released. Iterative privacy auditing is acknowledged to be potentially prohibitively expensive (motivating the weaker offline-LiRA), and offline-LiRA is by the authors' own admission a weaker privacy estimator than online-LiRA.

datasets_used: CIFAR10 (with ResNet18 architecture) for the preliminary iterative evaluation
quantitative_results: Online-LiRA (as implemented by the cited Kurmanji et al.) requires training 256 shadow models and running the unlearning algorithm 10,000 times per shadow model, totaling over 2.5 million unlearning runs per algorithm; the authors' offline-LiRA needs only a single unlearning run
Base ResNet18 model trained for 30 epochs; at each iteration the forget set Df is 1% of Dtrain
Per-algorithm hyperparameters found via 100 Optuna trials, optimized to minimize |0.5 - MIA Accuracy| and maximize validation accuracy
Iterative test accuracy tracked over roughly 40 unlearning iterations (Figure 1): BadTeach degrades to random guessing while others maintain or increase accuracy
Update-leakage: only some tested algorithms beat retraining-from-scratch, but none leaked extra information ('do no harm' satisfied)
baselines_compared: Finetune (finetune on retain set Dr, baseline)
